content:
# Luke Chapter 14 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 106-107 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_014_page_001.jpg, LUK_014_page_002.jpg

---

**1** One Sabbath, Jesus went to eat at the home of a prominent Pharisee, and the people there were watching him closely. **2** Right in front of him was a man whose body was swollen with fluid. **3** Jesus turned to the experts in the law and the Pharisees and asked, "Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?" **4** They said nothing. So he took hold of the man, healed him, and sent him on his way. **5** Then he said to them, "If your son or your ox falls into a well on the Sabbath, would you not immediately pull him out?" **6** And they had no answer to give him.

**7** When he noticed how the guests were jockeying for the seats of honor at the table, he told them this parable: **8** "When someone invites you to a wedding feast, do not take the seat of honor. Someone more distinguished than you may have been invited, **9** and the host who invited both of you will come and say to you, 'Give this person your seat.' Then, humiliated, you will have to move to the lowest place. **10** Instead, when you are invited, go and sit in the lowest place, so that when your host comes, he will say to you, 'Friend, move up to a better seat.' Then you will be honored in the presence of all the other guests. **11** For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and the one who humbles himself will be exalted."

**12** Then Jesus said to his host, "When you give a lunch or a dinner, do not invite your friends, your brothers, your relatives, or your wealthy neighbors — because they will return the favor by inviting you back, and that will be your repayment. **13** Instead, when you give a banquet, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, and the blind. **14** And you will be blessed, because they have no way to repay you. You will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous."

**15** When one of the guests at the table heard this, he said to Jesus, "How blessed is the person who will eat at the feast in the kingdom of God!" **16** Jesus replied: "A man was preparing a great banquet and invited many guests. **17** When everything was ready, he sent his servant to tell those who had been invited, 'Come, because everything is now prepared.' **18** But one after another, they all began making excuses. The first said, 'I have bought a field and I need to go see it. Please accept my apologies.' **19** Another said, 'I have bought five pairs of oxen and I am on my way to try them out. Please accept my apologies.' **20** Still another said, 'I have just gotten married, and for that reason I cannot come.'

**21** "The servant came back and reported all this to his master. Then the master of the house became angry and ordered his servant, 'Go out quickly into the streets and alleys of the city and bring in the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame.' **22** The servant said, 'Sir, what you ordered has been done, and there is still room.' **23** Then the master told the servant, 'Go out to the roads and the hedgerows and compel people to come in, so that my house will be full. **24** For I tell you, not one of those who were originally invited will get a taste of my banquet.'"

**25** Large crowds were traveling with Jesus, and he turned and said to them, **26** "If anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters — yes, even his own life — that person cannot be my disciple. **27** And whoever does not carry his own cross and follow me cannot be my disciple.

**28** "Suppose one of you wants to build a tower. Will you not first sit down and estimate the cost, to see whether you have enough to finish it? **29** Otherwise, if you lay the foundation and are unable to complete it, everyone who sees it will ridicule you, **30** saying, 'This person started to build and was not able to finish!'

**31** "Or suppose a king is about to go to war against another king. Will he not first sit down and consider whether with ten thousand soldiers he can stand against the one coming against him with twenty thousand? **32** If he cannot, then while the other king is still far away, he will send a delegation to ask for terms of peace. **33** In the same way, anyone who does not give up everything he has cannot be my disciple.

**34** "Salt is good, but if even salt loses its flavor, how can it be made salty again? **35** It is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile — it is thrown out. Let anyone who has ears to hear, listen!"

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **14:1 — "went to eat at the home of a prominent Pharisee"**: The Greek ARCHONTŌN TŌN PHARISAIŌN (archontōn tōn Pharisaiōn) means "a ruler of the Pharisees" — a leading figure, possibly a member of the Sanhedrin or a prominent community leader who also belonged to the Pharisaic party. The detail that they were PARATĒROUMENOI (paratēroumenoi, "watching closely") him reveals that this dinner is not genuine hospitality but a surveillance operation. The meal is a trap. The entire chapter unfolds at or departing from this dinner table, and every teaching Jesus delivers is shaped by the hypocrisy of the setting — religious leaders pretending to be hosts while actually functioning as inquisitors.

- **14:2 — "a man whose body was swollen with fluid"**: The condition is HYDRŌPIKOS (hydrōpikos), known in modern medicine as edema or dropsy — the abnormal accumulation of fluid in the body's tissues, causing visible swelling. In ancient medicine it was considered a serious and often fatal condition. In some rabbinic traditions, dropsy was seen as a punishment for sin (particularly sexual immorality), which adds a layer to the scene: the Pharisees may view this man as someone God has already judged. Whether this man was deliberately placed before Jesus as a test case (as the crippled woman was present in 13:10-11) or simply happened to be there, Luke does not say. But his placement "right in front of" (EMPROSTHEN, emprosthen) Jesus suggests something staged.

- **14:3-4 — "Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?"**: Jesus seizes the initiative by putting the question to them rather than waiting for them to challenge him. The silence (HĒSYCHASAN, hesychasan — "they were silent" or "they held their peace") is devastating. They cannot say "yes" without undermining their own legalistic framework; they cannot say "no" without appearing cruel in front of a suffering man. So they say nothing. Jesus heals the man and then releases him — APELYSEN (apelysen, "he let him go" or "he dismissed him"), the same vocabulary of liberation used in the healing of the bent woman in 13:12. Each Sabbath healing in Luke is a deliberate act of emancipation.

- **14:5 — "If your son or your ox falls into a well"**: The Sinaiticus reading here is YIOS Ē BOYS (huios ē bous, "a son or an ox"), which is the more difficult and likely original reading. Some later manuscripts change "son" to "donkey" (ONOS) to parallel the ox-and-donkey pairing in 13:15 and to avoid the jarring juxtaposition of a human child and an animal in the same sentence. But that jarring quality is precisely the point: if you would rescue even an animal on the Sabbath — how much more a human being? The argument moves from the lesser (an ox) to the greater (a son), making the case unanswerable. The Pharisees' silence in verse 6 confirms they have been completely outmaneuvered.

- **14:7-11 — The parable of the seating arrangement**: The word PARABOLĒN (parabolēn, "parable") is used, but what Jesus delivers is closer to practical wisdom in the tradition of Proverbs 25:6-7: "Do not put yourself forward in the king's presence or stand in the place of the great, for it is better to be told 'Come up here' than to be put lower in the presence of a noble." Jesus observes the guests choosing (EXELEGONTO, exelegonto) the PRŌTOKLISIAS (prōtoklisias, "chief reclining places") — the seats closest to the host, which indicated the highest social status. At formal Jewish meals, guests reclined on couches arranged in a U-shape, and position signaled rank. The teaching operates on two levels simultaneously: it is genuinely good social advice, but it is also a parable about the kingdom of God, where self-promotion leads to demotion and self-abasement leads to honor. The principle in verse 11 — identical to 18:14 — is one of Luke's central theological claims.

- **14:12-14 — "do not invite your friends... invite the poor"**: Jesus now turns from the guests to the host himself, and the teaching is radical. Ancient Mediterranean culture ran on reciprocity — the entire social fabric depended on an elaborate system of mutual obligation. You invited those who could invite you back; hospitality was an investment that yielded social returns. Jesus dismantles this system entirely. The four categories he names — the poor (PTŌCHOUS), the crippled (ANAPEIROUS), the lame (CHŌLOUS), and the blind (TYPHLOUS) — are precisely those excluded from temple worship in certain readings of Leviticus 21:18 and from the community at Qumran (the Dead Sea Scrolls explicitly bar the blind, lame, and disabled from the messianic banquet). Jesus commands that the people religion excludes from the feast are the very people who should be the guests of honor. The repayment will come at the ANASTASIS TŌN DIKAIŌN ("the resurrection of the righteous") — an eschatological promise that reframes all human generosity as investment in an eternal economy.

- **14:15 — "How blessed is the person who will eat at the feast in the kingdom of God!"**: This guest's exclamation is pious and comfortable — a safe theological platitude about the future messianic banquet. Everyone at the table would nod approvingly. But Jesus' response — the Parable of the Great Banquet — systematically destroys the assumption behind the statement. The man assumes he and his fellow guests are among those who will eat at that feast. Jesus is about to show that the confidently invited may be the ones who miss it entirely.

- **14:16-17 — "A man was preparing a great banquet"**: In the ancient Near East, banquet invitations were issued in two stages. The first invitation was sent well in advance — a "save the date" — and those who accepted entered into a social obligation to attend. The second summons came when everything was ready: "Come, for everything is now prepared" (DEUTE HOTI ĒDĒ HETOIMA ESTIN). To decline at this second stage was an extraordinary social insult — the host had already prepared food and arranged the event based on confirmed acceptances. Every refusal in what follows is therefore not merely an inconvenience but a deliberate affront.

- **14:18-20 — The three excuses**: Each excuse escalates in absurdity. The first man bought a field and needs to inspect it — but who buys land without inspecting it first? The purchase is already complete (ĒGORASA, "I bought"); the inspection is an afterthought used as a pretext. The second bought five yoke of oxen and needs to test them — five yoke represents a substantial farming operation, suggesting wealth, and again the purchase preceded the testing. The third simply says "I got married" — no apology, no pretense of regret. Under Deuteronomy 24:5, a newly married man was exempt from military service for one year, but a dinner invitation is not military duty. Each excuse reveals that worldly attachments — property, possessions, and relationships — take priority over the host's invitation. Luke's audience would hear in this parable the reasons people gave for declining the invitation of the gospel.

- **14:21-23 — "Go out... and bring in the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame"**: The master's response unfolds in two waves. First, the servant is sent into the streets (PLATEIAS) and alleys (RHYMAS) of the city — these are the urban poor, the marginalized of Israel. This mirrors Jesus' ministry to the outcasts within Judaism. When there is still room, a second wave goes beyond the city to the roads (HODOUS) and hedgerows (PHRAGMOUS) — the countryside margins where travelers, vagrants, and Gentiles might be found. The word ANANKASON (anankason, "compel" or "urge strongly") does not imply physical force but insistent, persuasive invitation. Augustine later used this verse to justify religious coercion, but the context is about overcoming the reluctance of people who cannot believe they are actually welcome at such a feast. The poor and broken must be convinced that the invitation is real.

- **14:24 — "not one of those who were originally invited will get a taste of my banquet"**: The master speaks in the first person — "MY banquet" (MOU TOU DEIPNOU) — and the shift from the parable's character to Jesus himself is unmistakable. This is now Jesus declaring that those who reject God's invitation will be excluded, not by divine caprice but by their own refusal. The verse functions as both the parable's conclusion and a prophetic warning. The originally invited guests — representing the religious establishment who assumed their place at the messianic table — have disqualified themselves. Their seats will be filled by those they would never have considered worthy.

- **14:26 — "does not hate his own father and mother"**: The word MISEI (misei, "hate") is one of the most startling words Jesus ever used. It does not mean emotional hostility or active malice. In Semitic idiom, "hate" can mean "love less" or "set aside in priority" — as in Malachi 1:2-3 ("Jacob I loved, Esau I hated") and Genesis 29:31 (Leah was "hated," meaning less favored). Nevertheless, Jesus deliberately chose the most extreme word available rather than a softer term. The rhetorical shock is intentional: following Jesus requires a loyalty so absolute that every other attachment — including the most sacred bonds of family and even one's own survival instinct (PSYCHĒN HEAUTOU, "his own life/soul") — must become secondary. The list of seven relationships (father, mother, wife, children, brothers, sisters, one's own life) is comprehensive and allows no exceptions.

- **14:27 — "carry his own cross"**: This is the second time in Luke that Jesus uses the cross metaphor for discipleship (the first was 9:23). For the original audience, this was not a spiritual metaphor — it was a reference to a specific Roman execution practice. Condemned prisoners were forced to carry the horizontal beam (patibulum) of their cross to the execution site, walking through the streets in public humiliation. To "carry your cross" meant to accept a path of public shame, suffering, and death. Jesus is saying that genuine discipleship looks like a dead man walking — someone who has already accepted the worst the world can do and follows anyway.

- **14:28-32 — The tower builder and the warring king**: These twin parables are often read as encouragement to "count the cost" of discipleship — and they are that. But notice the unexpected direction of the logic. The tower builder who cannot finish is ridiculed; the king who cannot win sues for peace. In both cases, the person who starts without adequate resources faces failure or surrender. Applied to discipleship, the message is sobering: Jesus does not want impulsive followers who will quit when the cost becomes clear. Better to honestly assess what is required and decide now than to start and abandon the project. The tower (PYRGON) may refer to a vineyard watchtower — a common agricultural construction project — rather than a military fortification, making the example deliberately ordinary. Even a small building project requires planning. How much more the decision to follow Jesus?

- **14:33 — "anyone who does not give up everything he has"**: The Greek APOTASSETAI (apotassetai) means "to say farewell to" or "to renounce" — it is the same word used when someone takes formal leave of another person (as in Acts 18:18). Jesus demands a complete renunciation of possessions — not merely a willingness to give them up but an actual farewell to them. This verse is the conclusion that the two parables have been building toward. The cost has been counted; the resources assessed. The answer is: you do not have enough on your own. Total surrender is the only viable strategy. You cannot negotiate partial terms with God as the weaker king negotiates peace. You must give up everything.

- **14:34-35 — "if even salt loses its flavor"**: Salt (HALAS) in the ancient world served multiple purposes: preserving food, flavoring meals, and — importantly for this context — fertilizing soil. Salt was mixed into manure piles to enhance their effectiveness as fertilizer, which explains Jesus' otherwise puzzling reference to the KOPRIA (kopria, "manure pile"). Salt that has lost its chemical potency (MŌRANTHĒ, mōranthē — literally "become foolish," the same root as the English "moron") is useless for any of these purposes. It cannot preserve, flavor, fertilize, or even improve compost. It is simply discarded. Applied to the disciples traveling with Jesus (v. 25), the warning is stark: a disciple who will not pay the cost of following — who will not prioritize Jesus over family, carry the cross, and renounce everything — is like salt without saltiness. Useless. The closing formula, "Let anyone who has ears to hear, listen!" (HO ECHŌN ŌTA AKOUEIN AKOUETŌ), signals that this teaching contains a deeper meaning that demands personal response. It is Luke's equivalent of an underline beneath the entire chapter.
